title: Reading and Use of English – Part 2
description: For questions 9–16, read the text below and think of the word which best fits each gap. Use only ONE word in each gap.

content.text: In the realm of archaeology, few things are (9) as captivating as uncovering traces of ancient civilizations. Archaeologists dedicate years to excavating sites, piecing together fragmented remnants (10) from the past.|| Yet, (11) is often not only the physical remnants they study, but (12) the social context and cultural practices. This holistic approach helps us understand how societies operated, maintained their structures, (13) adapted to various environmental challenges.|| Some discoveries are attributed to accidental findings, (14) others owe their existence to deliberate, methodical searches. Regardless of the method, each discovery can provide insight (15) into aspects of human history that were previously unknown.|| A prime example is the uncovering of Pompeii, preserved under layers of volcanic ash. This site offers a snapshot of ancient Roman life, capturing details (16) were frozen in time. Such discoveries not only enhance our historical comprehension but also challenge us to ponder the continuity between past and present civilizations.|| Therefore, understanding ancient cultures involves collaboration among various fields, illustrating (0) interconnected disciplines contribute valuable insights. This synthesis of knowledge not only enriches our grasp of history but also inspires future generations to preserve it. Through (9) insights gained, we can foster respect for (10) diversity of human experiences across millennia.

example.text: illustrating (0) interconnected disciplines contribute valuable insights.
example.explanation: Introduces a clause explaining the manner or way something is done.

questions.explanation: Used to express a comparison in a simile format.
Used to indicate the source or provenance of something.
Refers back to the singular noun 'remnants' as a pronoun.
Used to indicate addition or another scenario.
Used to present an alternative or additional possibility.
Used to contrast two different conditions or situations.
Used to suggest transformation or exploration.
Used as a relative pronoun to introduce a defining clause.
